title: Transit of Goods through Territory of Pakistan Order 2026: Six Land Routes, Third-Country Goods, and the Southeastern Bypass of Hormuz Pressure
subtitle: Gwadar, Taftan, Gabd, and the Multimodal Logic of Threshold-Delaying Supply

alternate_identifiers.identifier: Transit of Goods through Territory of Pakistan Order 2026 Six Land Routes, Third-Country Goods, and the Southeastern Bypass of Hormuz Pressure.pdf
Transit of Goods through Territory of Pakistan Order 2026
alternate_identifiers.description: Short form of the policy brief title

description: This policy brief examines Pakistan’s Transit of Goods through Territory of Pakistan Order 2026 as a southeastern logistics adaptation linked to Iran-bound third-country transit under Hormuz pressure. It analyzes six designated land routes, the 6+1+1+2 logistics architecture, potential China–Pakistan rail support, the Iran–Pakistan energy layer, and air–land and sea–land extensions, including a schematic map of the six designated Pakistan–Iran transit corridors.
abstract: This policy brief analyzes Pakistan’s Transit of Goods through Territory of Pakistan Order 2026, which designates six Iran-bound transit routes linking Karachi, Port Qasim, and Gwadar with Gabd and Taftan. The brief argues that the order does not replace Iran’s Persian Gulf maritime system, but institutionalizes a southeastern bypass capable of sustaining selective flows under pressure. Its core significance lies in third-country transit: external cargo can enter Pakistan through ports or airports and move onward to Iran by road, shifting the logistics problem from a narrow maritime chokepoint to a distributed system of ports, roads, borders, customs guarantees, financial documentation, and cargo prioritization. Building on EPINOVA–2026–PB–42’s three-layer model, the brief interprets the Pakistan corridor as a 6+1+1+2 architecture: six designated land routes, one potential China–Pakistan rail enabling layer, one Iran–Pakistan energy-infrastructure layer, and two intermodal extensions through air–land and sea–land movement. The analysis concludes that the corridor cannot reproduce Hormuz-scale maritime capacity, but can preserve minimum viable flow, support food and basic-goods continuity, maintain selected industrial inputs, and complicate blockade enforcement.

series_information.series_name: EPINOVA Policy Brief Series on Strategic Competition, AI-Enabled Warfare, and Information Conflict
series_information.series_description: Policy brief series translating EPINOVA research on strategic competition, AI-enabled warfare, information conflict, systemic risk, logistics resilience, and global security governance into concise policy-facing analysis.

methodology.source_basis: Policy-facing analysis based on Pakistan’s Transit of Goods through Territory of Pakistan Order 2026, public reporting by Dawn, ProPakistani, The Express Tribune, Reuters, and Global Energy Monitor, route geography, scenario-based throughput assumptions, EPINOVA–2026–PB–42’s three-layer logistics adaptation model, and EPINOVA–2026–PB–27’s constrained-capacity methodology.
methodology.case_context: Pakistan-based third-country transit to Iran during Hormuz pressure in the U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict, with emphasis on Karachi, Port Qasim, Gwadar, Gabd, Taftan, Balochistan road corridors, potential CPEC-linked rail support, air–land cargo movement, and the Iran–Pakistan energy layer.
methodology.core_problem: Hormuz pressure cannot be evaluated only through maritime traffic. Pakistan’s 2026 transit order shifts part of the Iran-bound logistics problem into a distributed system of ports, roads, borders, customs guarantees, documentation, trucking, insurance, financial controls, and potential multimodal extensions.
central_claims: Pakistan’s 2026 transit order institutionalizes third-country transit to Iran rather than merely expanding bilateral Pakistan–Iran trade.
The six designated routes create a southeastern port–road–border corridor linking Karachi, Port Qasim, and Gwadar with Gabd and Taftan.
The corridor delays pressure but does not replace Hormuz or reproduce Persian Gulf maritime scale.

The system is best understood as a 6+1+1+2 architecture: six designated routes, one potential China–Pakistan rail enabling layer, one latent Iran–Pakistan energy-infrastructure layer, and two intermodal extensions through air–land and sea–land movement.
The Iran–Pakistan gas pipeline is a latent energy-security layer rather than current freight capacity.
Pakistan functions as a southeastern logistics switchboard within Iran’s wider threshold-delaying supply system.

Blockade monitoring expands from vessels and Hormuz chokepoint traffic to Pakistan’s logistics surface, including ports, roads, border crossings, air-cargo intake points, bonded cargo systems, and third-country documentation.

analytical_lenses.name: Third-Country Transit Platform
analytical_lenses.description: Treats Pakistan as a legal and operational transit platform for goods originating outside Pakistan and moving toward Iran.

analytical_lenses.name: Six-Route Designated Corridor System
analytical_lenses.description: Analyzes the six legally designated routes linking Pakistani maritime entry points with Gabd and Taftan border exits.

analytical_lenses.name: 6+1+1+2 Architecture
analytical_lenses.description: Distinguishes the six formal routes from potential rail support, latent energy infrastructure, and air–land / sea–land extensions.

analytical_lenses.name: Threshold-Delaying Supply
analytical_lenses.description: Assesses whether limited but persistent flows can slow depletion, support food and basic-goods continuity, and maintain selected industrial inputs.

analytical_lenses.name: Constrained-Capacity Estimation
analytical_lenses.description: Uses scenario-based bounds rather than measured throughput to estimate likely sustained movement under port, road, customs, security, trucking, and border-processing constraints.

analytical_lenses.name: Friction Management under Porous Blockade
analytical_lenses.description: Frames the corridor as a mechanism that regionalizes and administratively complicates blockade pressure without eliminating it.

logistics_architecture.strategic_role: Creates a formal third-country transit framework

logistics_architecture.function: Rear-area cargo movement toward Pakistani ports and Balochistan corridors
logistics_architecture.strategic_role: Adds logistical depth if future rail upgrades mature

logistics_architecture.component: Iran–Pakistan gas pipeline / potential hydrocarbon layer
Pakistan air-cargo intake + road movement to border
Karachi / Port Qasim / Gwadar maritime intake + road exit to Iran
logistics_architecture.function: Third-country cargo enters Pakistan by sea and exits to Iran by road

capacity_summary.upper_constrained_range: 10,000–15,000 tons per day, equivalent to roughly 3.6–5.5 million tons per year under favorable constrained conditions
capacity_summary.method_note: Route-level estimates should not be mechanically summed because effective throughput is reduced by node bottlenecks, channel coupling, intermodal delays, road security, cargo documentation, customs clearance, bank guarantees, insurance, trucking coordination, and nonlinear failure dynamics.

key_findings: Pakistan’s 2026 transit order institutionalizes third-country transit to Iran, creating a legal framework for goods originating outside Pakistan to move through Pakistani territory toward Iran.
The six designated routes form a dual-border, multi-port, Balochistan-centered transit architecture rather than a single road corridor.
Karachi, Port Qasim, and Gwadar are linked to Gabd and Taftan, creating a southeastern port–road–border access layer outside direct Gulf entry.
The corridor does not replace Hormuz; it sustains limited flows of food, medicine, consumer goods, spare parts, industrial inputs, and selected high-value cargo.

The system is analytically described as a 6+1+1+2 architecture: six designated land routes, one potential China–Pakistan rail enabling layer, one Iran–Pakistan energy-infrastructure layer, and two intermodal extensions through air–land and sea–land movement.
Only the six land routes are formally designated by the 2026 order; the rail, energy, air–land, and sea–land components are analytical extensions or enabling layers.
The Iran–Pakistan gas pipeline is a latent long-term energy-security layer and should not be counted as immediate goods throughput.

The corridor’s working capacity is likely low-to-medium and constrained by port clearance, trucking availability, road security in Balochistan, border processing, customs procedures, insurance, bank guarantees, and documentation requirements.

Pakistan’s highest practical value is likely as a third-country transit platform for selective sea–land or air–land movement, especially for China, Southeast Asia, Gulf re-export hubs, and humanitarian suppliers.
The corridor regionalizes Hormuz pressure and shifts enforcement from maritime chokepoint monitoring to a wider port–road–border and documentation problem.
Pakistan is becoming a southeastern logistics switchboard within Iran’s wider threshold-delaying supply system.
policy_recommendations: Shift monitoring from maritime traffic alone to logistics-system indicators, including Karachi, Port Qasim, Gwadar, Gabd, Taftan, Quetta / Lakpass, Dalbandin, Nokundi, bonded cargo movements, air-cargo intake, port congestion, and border-clearance rates.

Focus pressure analysis on bottlenecks rather than whole corridors, especially customs interfaces, port clearance systems, border exits, fuel availability, trucking capacity, insurance, and bank guarantees.

Interpret air–land movement as a critical-input channel rather than a bulk-trade solution; its value lies in medicine, electronics, communications equipment, precision components, and time-sensitive parts.
Treat China–Pakistan rail connectivity as a future enabling layer rather than a current Iran-bound corridor unless formal linkage or operational evidence appears.
Treat the Iran–Pakistan gas pipeline as a long-term energy-security variable with high strategic relevance but limited current operational contribution to logistics capacity.

Evaluate porous logistics adaptation by throughput, delivery time, cost, documentation burden, cargo prioritization, and enforcement complexity rather than by the binary question of whether goods still move.

figures.title: Pakistan–Iran Transit Corridors, 2026
figures.description: Schematic map of the six designated Pakistan–Iran transit routes linking Karachi, Port Qasim, and Gwadar with Gabd and Taftan under the Transit of Goods through Territory of Pakistan Order 2026.
figures.source: Author’s reconstruction based on Pakistan’s Transit of Goods through Territory of Pakistan Order 2026 and public reporting
figures.note: Approximate schematic map for analytical use; not for navigation.

tables.title: Pakistan’s Six Designated Iran-Bound Transit Routes
tables.description: Lists the six designated routes linking Pakistani maritime intake points with Gabd and Taftan, including main nodes, border exits, and primary functions.
tables.source: Pakistani reporting on the 2026 transit order, including Dawn and ProPakistani

tables.title: Pakistan–Iran Southeastern Logistics Architecture
tables.description: Analytical 6+1+1+2 architecture including six designated land routes, China–Pakistan rail potential, Iran–Pakistan energy connectivity, and air–land and sea–land extensions.
tables.source: Author’s analytical framework based on Pakistani reporting, PB-42’s three-layer model, and public information on China–Pakistan/CPEC rail potential and the Iran–Pakistan gas pipeline

tables.title: Indicative Route Capacity and Cargo Fit
tables.description: Scenario-based estimates of sustained route capacity, annualized range, best cargo fit, and strategic role for the six designated routes.
tables.source: Author’s scenario estimates based on the six designated routes, route geography, truck-based throughput assumptions, port–road–border constraints, and EPINOVA PB-27 constrained-capacity methodology

tables.title: Cargo Types and Corridor Suitability
tables.description: Assesses likely cargo categories, modal suitability, and strategic value for Pakistan-based Iran transit.
tables.source: Author’s assessment based on route geography, modal suitability, Pakistan’s six designated transit routes, PB-42’s land–sea and air-replenishment distinction, and PB-27’s constrained-capacity logic

tables.title: Potential Source Countries and Regions for Pakistan-Based Iran Transit
tables.description: Identifies plausible source countries and regions for Pakistan-based Iran transit, likely Pakistan entry points, Iran exits, cargo fit, and main constraints.
tables.source: Author’s assessment based on the third-country transit logic of the 2026 order, Pakistan’s port and border geography, trade-source compatibility, and sanctions/documentation constraints

disclaimer: This policy brief is an institutional publication of EPINOVA, prepared by Dr. Shaoyuan Wu in his capacity as Director of the Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC. The analysis is based on publicly available information and scenario-based analytical estimates and does not represent the official position of any government. The publication is intended solely for research and policy discussion purposes and does not constitute legal, military, operational, or sanctions-compliance advice.
